topic: A person finds out their partner has been learning their native language
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the unfamiliar cadence, sharp and melodic, cut through the quiet of the apartment like a poorly tuned instrument. I was folding laundry, a mundane task I usually found soothing, but today, every movement felt weighted. I glanced over at Liam, who was sitting at the dining table, intensely focused on a textbook, muttering phrases under his breath that were definitely not English. My jaw tightened, the muscles in my neck corded. When he finally looked up, a small, triumphant smile played on his lips, and the look in his eyes—that bright, knowing sparkle—made my stomach clench. "Oh, hey," I managed, my voice coming out flat, deliberately devoid of warmth. I picked up a sock, smoothing out a crease with unnecessary force. I needed a minute to recalibrate.

[story 2]

It started subtly—a slip of a word here, a slight inflection there, tucked into casual conversation. Now, it was a full immersion. I caught him practicing in the kitchen late last night, the sounds rich and flowing, completely foreign to our usual evening soundtrack. I stood by the doorway, arms crossed so tightly my knuckles were white. I watched him articulate a complex sentence, the sounds rolling off his tongue with an effortless grace that felt like a deliberate challenge. When he finished, he beamed, clearly pleased with his progress. I just stared at the wall, the silence in my chest growing heavy and pressurized. I walked into the living room and deliberately poured myself a glass of water, taking a slow, measured sip, trying to keep my expression neutral as a deep furrow settled between my brows.

[story 3]

I found the evidence tucked inside his laptop—a language learning app open to a complex grammar lesson, annotated with notes in a script I didn't recognize. The discovery landed with a dull thud in my chest. I walked over, my steps measured and slow, stopping just short of invading his space. "What is this?" I asked, my tone clipped, like I was reading a difficult instruction manual. He jumped slightly, his practiced composure momentarily fracturing. He looked up, and the flush creeping up his neck confirmed my suspicions. He started to explain, his voice quick and eager, trying to justify the activity. I just shook my head once, a small, sharp movement, and turned away, focusing intently on rearranging the books on the shelf, every gesture precise and stiff.
